unitTitle: KS2 Y5 Video production (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: What is video?
lessons.objectives:
I can say that a video is moving pictures, often with sound
I can spot a talking head and a close up in a video
I can compare two videos and say how they are different
I know to tell a trusted adult if something online upsets me
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: video · audio · camera · talking head · close up. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; one short, calm clip ready to play (teacher-played, low volume). Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what is video? Pupils answer on the starter worksheet: a video is moving pictures, usually with sound. Do not over-prompt — we revisit it at the end. Likely error: “a video is a photo.” Fix-words: “a photo is still, a video MOVES.”
   • Support: pick which one is a video.  • Core: tick what you see/hear in a video.  • Challenge: one way a video differs from a photo.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — shots. Play ONE short clip (pause after ~2 min). Point out a talking head (wide) and a close up (face). Pupils MATCH the shots on the activity worksheet.
3. (20–30) Compare — show a second, different clip (e.g. a music video). Think–pair–share: how is it different? Pupils write one difference.
4. (30–45) Staying safe online. Videos have age ratings; some are not for children. If something online upsets you, tell a trusted adult. Keep it calm; link to the school’s online-safety rules. Pupils answer the safety questions on the activity worksheet.
5. (45–55) Plenary — revisit “what is a video?”; celebrate improved answers; self-tick the “I can…”. Each pupil points to a talking head and a close up.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/animation; clips teacher-played and low volume; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Filming techniques
lessons.objectives:
I can find the lens, screen and record button on my filming device
I can film using different shots
I can match a shot to what it is good for
I can spot shots used in a video
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: video camera · lens · record · close up · long shot · high angle · low angle. TA: starter worksheet out; one filming device per pair; film in landscape. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — get to know your device. Pupils turn it on/off, record a few seconds, play it back. Pupils LABEL the camera (lens, record light, screen) on the starter worksheet. Likely error: filming in portrait. Fix-words: “turn it sideways — landscape.”
   • Support: which part is the lens.  • Core: drag the three labels.  • Challenge: why the screen showing the shot is useful.
2. (5–25) I-do → you-do — shots. Demonstrate close up, long shot, side by side; pupils try them in pairs (practice only, not the project). TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
3. (25–35) Camera angles. High = looks smaller; low = looks bigger; normal = real size. Pupils SORT the angles on the activity worksheet.
4. (35–45) Which technique? Pupils MATCH each shot to what it shows on the activity worksheet, then watch a short clip (teacher-played) and spot the shots used. Review together.
5. (45–55) Plenary — each pupil names one shot and what it is good for; self-tick the “I can…”. Save practice clips.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation; clips teacher-played and low volume; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Using a storyboard
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a storyboard is
I can suggest a technique for a purpose
I can film a short video from a storyboard
I can review how good my video is
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: storyboard · static camera · zoom · pan · tilt. TA: starter worksheet out; devices ready; the example storyboard to hand. Recap last lesson’s shots. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is a storyboard? A plan that shows each shot BEFORE you film (picture, shot type, words). Pupils answer on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: when you make a storyboard.  • Core: what it helps you do.  • Challenge: why planning shots first helps.
2. (10–20) I-do — three new techniques. Static camera (stays still), zoom (bigger/smaller, camera still), pan and tilt (turns to follow). Pupils MATCH them on the activity worksheet. Likely error: walking while filming a “static” shot. Fix-words: “static means the camera does not move.”
3. (20–40) You-do — film the given storyboard. Groups of 2–3; everyone films and is filmed (unless agreed). Pupils ORDER the filming steps on the activity worksheet. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (40–50) Review. Pupils RATE how helpful the storyboard was (slider) and write one improvement on the activity worksheet. Keep it kind and short.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the “I can…”; each group shows one scene. Next lesson: plan your OWN video.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation; clips teacher-played and low volume; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Planning a video
lessons.objectives:
I can say what goes in a storyboard box
I can choose a theme and plan my scenes on a storyboard
I can film and save my first scene
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: storyboard · scene · script · save. TA: starter worksheet out; the storyboard handout; devices ready. This starts the real project. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what goes in a storyboard box? A picture of the shot, the shot type, and the words. Pupils answer on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: what you draw in a box.  • Core: tick what goes in a box.  • Challenge: why plan all scenes first.
2. (5–15) Choose your theme. A short ‘piece to camera’ with a prop, like the Lesson 1 example. Keep themes realistic and props easy. Pupils write their theme on the activity worksheet.
3. (15–35) Create your storyboard. First three boxes are started; pupils plan the rest. Likely error: too many scenes. Fix-words: “keep it short — a few good scenes.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (35–50) Film and save your first scene. Film → watch back → film again if needed → SAVE. Pupils ORDER the plan → film → save steps on the activity worksheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — quick review (clear picture? clear sound? matches storyboard?); self-tick the “I can…”. Reassure: more time next lesson. If props allowed, remind pupils to bring them.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation; clips teacher-played and low volume; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Importing and editing video
lessons.objectives:
I can import my video onto the computer
I can find the tools in the video editor
I can decide whether to reshoot or edit
I can split and trim a clip
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: import · clip · split · trim · reshoot · edit. TA: starter worksheet out; devices and the editing program ready; a labelled folder for files. First, give pupils time to film any scenes they still need. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the video editor. Library (clips), preview (plays), timeline (clips in order), tools (trim/split). Pupils LABEL the editor on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: where the video plays.  • Core: drag the four labels.  • Challenge: why clips must be on the timeline to edit.
2. (10–25) Import your clips. Copy clips from the camera into a labelled folder, then into the editor. Pupils ORDER the import steps on the activity worksheet. Likely error: losing the files. Fix-words: “one labelled folder for everything.”
3. (25–40) Reshoot or edit? Edit small problems (boring bit, wobble, wrong order); reshoot big ones (wrong way, someone walked in, forgot the words). A video producer decides. Pupils SORT the problems on the activity worksheet.
4. (40–50) Split and trim. Model split (cut a clip in two) then trim (cut off the start/end). Likely error: cutting before watching. Fix-words: “watch first, then cut.” Pupils try trimming one clip; SAVE.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the “I can…”; check everyone’s clips are imported and saved.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation; editor screen calm, no auto-play sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Video evaluation
lessons.objectives:
I can trim and reorder my clips to improve my video
I can export my video as a file
I can evaluate my video and say one good thing and one to improve
I can think about how to share a video safely
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: delete · trim · reorder · export · evaluate · share. TA: starter worksheet out; each group’s clips ready in the editor. Last lesson of the unit. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — editing tools. Delete (whole clip), trim (part of a clip), reorder (new order). Pupils MATCH the tools on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: which tool removes a whole clip.  • Core: match all three tools.  • Challenge: best tool to remove a spare take.
2. (10–30) Finish your video (you-do). Delete unwanted takes, trim wobbly/boring bits, put clips in storyboard order. Likely error: clips out of order. Fix-words: “match the storyboard order.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
3. (30–40) Export. Save keeps the editable project; export makes one finished mp4 that plays anywhere. Pupils ORDER the final steps on the activity worksheet, then export.
4. (40–50) Evaluate and share. Pupils RATE clarity (slider) and write one good thing and one kind improvement. Discuss sharing safely: ask the people in the video first; ask a teacher; keep it private if unsure. Pupils tick the safe choices on the activity worksheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — celebrate the whole journey (what is video → shots → storyboard → plan → film → edit → export); self-tick the “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; the evaluation is a shared task everyone does. No flashing/animation; clips teacher-played and low volume; movement breaks are routine.
